title: Writing Promotion Packets & Career Growth
description: How Staff+ engineers write promotion packets that get approved at FAANG. Covers the promotion packet structure, impact quantification, peer feedback collection, the promotion committee review process, writing for yourself vs writing for others, and real packet excerpts from SAP-to-FAANG transitions.

description.value:
A FAANG promotion packet makes the case that an engineer is performing at the next level. The packet is reviewed by a promotions committee (senior engineers from across the company) who do not know the candidate personally. The packet must stand on its own — the committee should reach the same conclusion without additional context from the candidate's manager or sponsor.

Standard packet structure: (1) Promotion statement (1 paragraph): 'I recommend X for promotion to L6 (Staff Software Engineer). X has consistently operated at the L6 level for the last 6 months, demonstrating impact across the payments platform through architectural leadership, mentoring, and incident reduction.' (2) Impact summary (3-5 bullet points, quantified): 'Led the payment reliability initiative, reducing P99 latency from 800ms to 100ms, saving $10M/year in abandoned carts. Designed the idempotency key framework used by 5 teams. Mentored 3 L5 engineers to L6 readiness. Established the payments on-call rotation, reducing P0 incidents by 80%.' (3) Key projects (2-3 detailed narratives): For each project: problem → approach → result → impact. Use the STAR format. Each narrative should demonstrate next-level scope (cross-team, organizational impact). (4) Peer feedback (4-6 quotes from L6+ engineers): Colleagues at and above the target level testify to the candidate's impact. The feedback must reference specific projects and behaviors, not general praise. (5) Growth trajectory (1 paragraph): What will the candidate do at the next level? What is their vision for the next 12 months? The committee needs to see that the promotion is not just a reward for past work but an investment in future impact.

Quantifying impact for SAP engineers: SAP engineers often describe impact in system-level terms ('I migrated 2000 custom Z-programs'). FAANG committees want business-impact terms: 'I migrated 2000 custom Z-programs to standard S/4HANA, reducing the customization footprint by 40% and saving the company $2M/year in license and maintenance costs.' Translate every technical achievement into business value: hours saved, revenue enabled, incidents prevented, costs reduced.

sections.title: The Promotion Committee — What Happens Behind Closed Doors

description.value:
The promotion committee (PC) is a group of 3-5 L6+ engineers from different parts of the company. They review 5-10 packets per meeting. Each packet gets 15-30 minutes. The committee does not know the candidate personally — they evaluate solely on the written packet. This is why the packet must be self-contained.

What the committee looks for: (1) Sustained performance, not one-time heroics. One great project is not enough. The committee wants to see 3+ examples of next-level work over 6-18 months. (2) Cross-team impact. At L6+, impact must extend beyond the candidate's immediate team. The committee looks for evidence of influence across 2+ teams. (3) Organizational scaling. The candidate made their team and adjacent teams more effective. Mentoring, design standards, on-call improvements, tech debt reduction. (4) Level-appropriate scope. For L5 to L6: leading multi-team initiatives, defining architectural direction, mentoring L5 engineers. For L6 to L7: setting org-wide technical direction, creating frameworks used by 10+ teams, growing Staff+ engineers.

How committees decide: The committee chair reads the packet aloud. Members ask clarifying questions. Each member gives an initial rating. If there is disagreement, the committee debates. The manager and sponsor are not in the room — their job was to write a convincing packet. Common outcomes: Approve (rare on first review), Approve with conditions (e.g., 'Promote in 3 months after completing project X'), Defer (needs more evidence, resubmit in 6 months), Decline (evidence does not demonstrate next-level performance).

sections.title: Writing Packets for Others — The Sponsorship Skill

description.value:
Writing promotion packets for your team members is one of the highest-impact activities a Staff+ engineer can do. It signals that you invest in growing others — a requirement for reaching L7+. It also demonstrates that your impact scales through the people you develop.

Key principles for writing someone else's packet: (1) Start 3 months before the submission deadline. Collect evidence (project impact numbers, peer feedback, design doc links, performance review highlights) continuously throughout the year, not in a panic during the last week. (2) Let the candidate write the first draft — they know their work best. You edit and strengthen the narrative, particularly for impact quantification and level-appropriate framing. (3) Highlight patterns, not just projects. The committee looks for sustained performance at the next level — one great project is not enough. Show 3+ examples over 6+ months. (4) Address weaknesses proactively. If the candidate has a gap (e.g., limited cross-team collaboration), show how they have started closing it. 'The candidate identified cross-team visibility as a growth area. They initiated a monthly cross-team design review that now includes 4 teams.' (5) Socialize the packet with the candidate's stakeholders before submission. Ensure the endorsements are consistent with the packet's claims. A surprise objection from a stakeholder during the committee review can kill a promotion.

The packet reflects the writer: A well-written packet for a team member who gets promoted demonstrates that the writer (you) can grow talent. A poorly written packet (vague impact claims, unclear narratives, missing evidence) reflects poorly on the writer. Invest the time as if your own reputation is on the line — because it is. When you write a promotion packet for someone, their success is your success.

sections.title: Peer Feedback — How to Collect and Use It

description.value:
Peer feedback is the most persuasive part of a promotion packet. The committee knows that the manager and sponsor are biased. They look to peer feedback for independent validation. Strong peer feedback can save a borderline packet. Weak peer feedback can sink a strong packet.

Who to ask: (1) Engineers at or above the target level — an L5 recommendation for L6 is weaker than an L6+ recommendation. (2) Engineers from different teams — demonstrates cross-team impact. (3) Engineers who have worked with the candidate on specific projects — they can provide concrete examples. (4) Engineers who are credible writers — vague, generic feedback ('X is a great engineer') is useless. The committee needs specific, behavioral feedback.

What to ask for: Instead of 'Can you write a recommendation?' use a structured request: 'I am supporting X's promotion to L6. Could you provide a brief recommendation focusing on: (1) a specific project where you worked with X, (2) what X did that demonstrated L6-level impact, (3) the business impact of X's contribution, (4) one area where X could continue to grow? Please be specific — the committee values concrete examples over general praise.'

How many: 4-6 peer feedback quotes. At least 2 from engineers at the target level or above. At least 2 from engineers outside the candidate's immediate team. Diversity of perspectives is more important than volume.

sections.codeBlock:
# Peer Feedback Request Template

Subject: Supporting [Name]'s promotion to [Level]

Hi [Name],

I am supporting [Candidate]'s promotion to [Level]. Given that you worked closely with them on [Project], I would value your perspective.

Could you provide a brief recommendation covering:

1. A specific project or situation where you worked with [Candidate]
2. What they did that demonstrated [Target Level] behaviors or impact
3. The measurable business impact of their contribution
4. One area where they could continue to grow (optional but valuable)

Concrete examples are more helpful than general praise — the promotion committee values specific evidence.

I need this by [Date]. The recommendation should be 2-4 paragraphs.

Thank you for supporting this promotion!

[Your Name]

adversarialResponse.challenge: "My impact is hard to quantify. I work on infrastructure/platform teams — we don't have revenue numbers or customer-facing metrics. How do I write a promotion packet without traditional business metrics?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "You must find a way to attach dollar values to your work. The committee requires quantified impact." (unhelpful, creates panic)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Platform and infrastructure teams can use alternative quantification methods: (1) Developer velocity — 'Reduced CI build time from 45min to 12min, saving 500 engineer-hours/month across 8 teams.' (2) Incident reduction — 'Reduced P0 incidents in the deployment pipeline by 90% through automated rollback and canary analysis.' (3) Cost efficiency — 'Reduced cloud costs by $2M/year through rightsizing and reserved instance optimization.' (4) Scale enablement — 'Designed the service mesh that supports 200 microservices and 10K requests/second, enabling 5 new feature teams to operate independently.' If you truly cannot find any numbers, use directional quantification: 'Before: teams spent 2 weeks setting up a new service. After my platform work: teams can self-serve in 2 hours.' The committee understands that platform work is harder to quantify — they look for proxy metrics (adoption rate, feedback scores, time saved per team) and narrative coherence (clear before/after comparison)."
adversarialResponse.pattern: alternative-quantification — platform and infrastructure work requires different metrics: velocity, reliability, cost, scale. The story matters as much as the numbers. A clear before/after narrative with any reasonable metric is better than a vague claim of impact.

description.value:
Each FAANG company has a different promotion culture and process. Understanding these differences helps you tailor your packet and manage your expectations.

Google: Promotions are primarily manager-driven. The manager writes the packet and presents to the promotion committee. The committee is strict about evidence — vague claims without supporting data are rejected. Google has a 'promotion quota' — a limited number of promotions per org per cycle. Even strong candidates may be deferred due to quota. SAP-specific challenge: Google values 'technical depth' and 'Googleyness' (cultural fit). SAP engineers who can demonstrate open-source contributions or distributed systems knowledge have an advantage.

Meta (Facebook): Promotions are peer-driven — the candidate drives their own packet with support from peers and manager. The packet includes extensive peer feedback (8-12 quotes). Meta values 'impact' above all else — the committee asks: 'Did this person's work meaningfully move the business metrics?' Meta has a faster promotion cadence than Google — strong performers can be promoted every 12-18 months. SAP-specific challenge: Meta expects candidates to have shipped products used by millions. SAP engineers should frame their impact in terms of users and business outcomes.

Amazon: Promotions are based on the 'Leveling Guidelines' — a detailed document describing what L5, L6, L7 looks like at Amazon. The candidate must demonstrate they have operated at the next level for at least 6 months. Amazon uses 'Promotion Documents' (similar to Google's packet) and 'OLR' (Organizational Level Review) where senior leaders review all promotions in an org. Amazon values the 'Leadership Principles' — every promotion claim must tie back to specific leadership principles (Customer Obsession, Ownership, Dive Deep, etc.). SAP-specific challenge: Amazon's 'Bias for Action' principle can conflict with SAP's methodical, process-driven culture. Show that you can move fast while maintaining quality.

Apple: The least transparent promotion process. Promotions are manager-driven but the criteria are not publicly documented. Apple values 'craftsmanship' and 'ownership.' Promotions are often tied to shipping a major product or feature. SAP-specific challenge: Apple's secrecy culture makes it hard to benchmark. Focus on building strong relationships with your manager and skip-level.

sections.title: Ongoing Career Growth Beyond the Packet

description.value:
A promotion packet is a snapshot of work you have already done. If you start preparing the packet 1 month before the deadline, you have waited too long. Career growth at FAANG is a continuous process of: (1) doing next-level work, (2) documenting that work, (3) making that work visible, and (4) getting feedback on whether that work demonstrates next-level performance.

The 'brag document' practice: Keep a running document of your achievements. Every month, add 2-3 bullet points describing your impact. Include: the problem, what you did, the measurable outcome. This document serves as: (a) raw material for your promotion packet, (b) input for your self-review, (c) talking points for your 1:1s with your manager. Start this from day 1 at the company.

The 'gap assessment' conversation: Every quarter, ask your manager: 'I am targeting L6 promotion in 12 months. Where am I relative to that bar? What are the 3 biggest gaps I need to close?' This gives you a clear, actionable growth plan. If your manager can't answer, they are not invested in your promotion — that is critical information that helps you decide whether to stay, switch teams, or find a sponsor.

The 'visibility gap': Many engineers do great work but their promotion fails because the committee has never heard of them. Closing the visibility gap: (1) Present at engineering reviews and all-hands. (2) Write internal blog posts about your projects. (3) Share design docs with senior engineers across the company. (4) Volunteer for cross-team initiatives. (5) Mentor engineers from other teams. Visibility is not optional for promotion — it is evidence that your impact extends beyond your team.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: The question 'Tell me about a time you helped someone get promoted' tests sponsorship. A strong answer: 'I sponsored X for L6 promotion. I started preparing their packet 3 months before the deadline. I helped them identify 3 projects that demonstrated L6-level impact across their team and adjacent teams. I collected peer feedback from 6 engineers at L6 and above. The packet was approved in the first review.' Show systematic sponsorship, not just 'I wrote a recommendation letter.' For SAP engineers, the story of building an ABAP training program or mentoring junior developers maps directly to this interview question — frame it as sponsorship, not just teaching.
